content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

December 31, 1975

Mr. Robert L. Holt
Member, Board of Directors
San Clemente Homeowners Association
229 W. Escalones
San Clemente, CA 92672

Dear Mr. Holt:

Thank you for your letter of December 17, 1975, regarding
conflicts of interest in the deliberations of your local
government.

You asked whether the law is satisfied if the public
servant declares the conflict of interest and abstains from
voting on matters that would amount to a direct conflict of
interest. The conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act apply not only to voting but to all participation
by the public official. Government Code Section 87100 pro-
hibits every public official from making, participating in the
making of in any way attempting to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has
reason to know he has a financial interest. The meaning of
the term "financial interest" is set forth in Gov. Code
Section 87103. The Fair Political Practices Commission
currently is considering regulations which clarify the mean-
ing of the statutory section. A copy of those draft regula-
tions is enclosed, and I direct your attention particularly
to proposed Sections 18700(b), (c) and (d). As you can see,
these regulations include a broad spectrum of conduct within
the meaning of "making governmental decisions," "participating
in the making of governmental decisions," and "in any way
attempting to use his or her official position."

In answer to your second question, local government has
the right to make the pertinent law more stringent, as long
as the local law does not prevent a person from complying
with the requirements of the Political Reform Act. Gov. Code
Section 81013.

I hope the foregoing information will be helpful. Please
contact Del Spurlock, Chief of the Conflicts of Interest Divi-
sion, at (916) 322-6441, if you have any questions.

Sincerely,

Natalie D. West,
Staff Counsel

NEW:PVP

sections.question: You asked whether the law is satisfied if the public servant declares the conflict of interest and abstains from voting on matters that would amount to a direct conflict of interest.
sections.conclusion: The conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act apply not only to voting but to all participation by the public official. Government Code Section 87100 prohibits every public official from making, participating in the making or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a public official satisfy conflict of interest requirements by merely declaring the conflict and abstaining from voting on directly conflicting matters?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mere abstention from voting is insufficient; the Political Reform Act prohibits all forms of participation and influence, not just voting, requiring public officials to avoid any involvement in matters involving their financial interests.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document addresses two questions; second question regarding local government authority to impose stricter rules also answered. Draft regulations referenced but not included in text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked whether the law is satisfied if the public servant declares the conflict of interest and abstains from voting on matters that would amount to a direct conflict of interest.

CONCLUSION: The conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act apply not only to voting but to all participation by the public official. Government Code Section 87100 prohibits every public official from making, participating in the making or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

Government Code Section 87100 pro- hibits every public official from making, participating in the making of in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that conflict of interest laws require public officials to abstain from all participation—not just voting—in governmental decisions involving their financial interests. The response clarifies that declaration and vote abstention alone do not satisfy the Political Reform Act.
